title: Pucks In Depth: bLue - A Career In Retrospect
subtitle: Whether this is the end of the story, or simply the beginning of a new chapter, bLue's career will be one to remember forever.
author: EggsThink

body.text: With less than a minute remaining in game 4 of the Season 17 Pro Finals, the score remained deadlocked at 5 goals apiece.

Every single touch of the puck carried the weight of an entire season, every little slip up threatened to become a permanent part of SPL history. What seemed like a harmless clearing attempt from [[player:Jagr|Jagr]] would become one of these moments as [[player:chaos|Chaos]] intercepted the puck before tossing it in front of the net, where the ever so hungry [[player:bLue|bLue]] would win a battle in front of the net, beating [[player:someone|Sum1]] for his 2nd goal of the game with just 52 seconds left in the period.

As the last 3 seconds ticked away, [[player:Milk|Milk]] would rip a shot off the post, as the [[player:new_england_stars|New England Star]]’s home crowd erupted and the [[player:long_island_lightning|Lightning]] players just stared on in disbelief.

For most players, a goal like [[player:bLue|bLue]]’s would define a career, but for him it was just another Monday night as he added yet another piece of hardware to his trophy case. Even many seasons after, fans remember that goal. However, what many forget is the long journey that led to here, a journey marked by perseverance, overcoming obstacles and the growth of a player who became one of, if not the most important competitor in SPL’s history.
This is the story of [[player:bLue|bLue]]’s SPL career.
the birth of the pacific king

On February 18th, 2021 a little known player named [[player:bLue|bLue]] would make his SPL debut with the [[team:victoria_braves|Victoria Braves]] alongside [[player:unknown_player|Kush]], [[player:dr_samurai|DrSamurai]] and [[player:tylerexe|Tyler.exe]] in the first season of Rebound on the West Coast. [[player:bLue|bLue]] would finish his first ever season with 23 goals and 31 points. Nothing too crazy, but he still managed to catch the eyes of scouts on the East Coast,
[[player:bLue|bLue]] is actually crazy, some pro or chal team pick him up.
From here on, it wouldn’t take long for [[player:bLue|bLue]] ’s career to start taking off.
Season 2: Eastbound

[[player:bLue|bLue]] and [[player:kylar|Kylar]] would unite to form the [[team:maui_monkeys|Maui Monkeys]] on West alongside [[player:hotwinglol|Hotwing]]. For the longest time, wherever [[player:bLue|bLue]] went, [[player:Kylar|Kylar]] was not very far behind as the two formed an almost unbreakable bond. [[player:kylar|Kylar]] passed the puck and [[player:bLue|bLue]] crashed. [[player:bLue|bLue]]’s [[team:maui_monkeys|Monkeys]] would finish 2nd in the regular season behind his mentor [[player:tylerexe|Tyler.exe]] and the [[team:yosemite_bobcats|Bobcats]]. This is where [[player:bLue|bLue]] would get his first taste of winning, sweeping the [[team:halifax_hydras|Hydras]] in dominant fashion in the Finals, bringing home the first ever official Pacific Cup Championship. At the same time [[player:bLue|bLue]] was winning in West, he was tearing up the Intermediate division on the East Coast, putting up a total of 110 goals a number that seems unfathomable even today. As a West player, [[player:bLue|bLue]] had to battle not just his opponents but the servers as well. Despite that disadvantage, [[player:bLue|bLue]] proved he belonged among SPL’s elites.
So what was next for [[player:bLue|bLue]]?
Season 3: another ring for the king

[[player:bLue|bLue]] would make his Pro debut alongside the Tampa Bay Turtles with 50 points in 14 games, all while maintaining his dominance in West, bringing home yet another Pacific Cup Championship.
Season 4: flipping the script

[[player:bLue|bLue]] would make SPL history as the first West-based player to lead the Pro division in goals and points with 75 goals and 95 points. Despite his ping roadblock, he continued to prove he could compete with anyone in the game. [[player:bLue|bLue]] would unfortunately lose to the eventual champions in the quarterfinals. For seasons, West players had been viewed as talented but limited by ping. [[player:bLue|bLue]]'s Season 4 shattered that perception.
[[player:Jagr]] believes one word best defines [[player:bLue|bLue]]'s career: Passion.
He is one of the few people willing and able to play all servers and be good on all of them.
That willingness to compete anywhere and against anyone became a defining theme of [[player:blue|bLue]]'s career. He wasn't just competing with the East's best players, he was outperforming them.
Season 5: against all odds

The following season, [[player:bLue|bLue]] would make it even closer to the coveted prize, as he and [[player:kylar|Kylar]] still attached at the hip would find their way to the Pro Finals before getting swept by the powerhouse [[team:pittsburgh_vipers|Pittsburgh Vipers]].
When asked about playing alongside Kylar, [[player:bLue|bLue]] was quick to praise his longtime teammate.
I still fully believe he was the smartest player to play this game. His game sense was unreal and made it simple for me. There was just something different about Kylar.
He went on to add,

To this day I still believe he is the best 3 that I have ever played with, and that is not a shot at any of the other 3s I’ve played with over the years, he just set such a high standard of what it takes to play that position and in my opinion no one has reached that standard yet.

Having fallen short multiple times now, [[player:bLue|bLue]] was now due for a Pro Championship, the duo of Kyler and Kylar would be formed alongside [[player:bLue|bLue]] under the [[team:plymouth_plague|Plymouth Plague]] name. Entering the playoffs as the 4th seed, this trio would come alive in the playoffs as they would go on to win a Pro Championship. [[player:bLue|bLue]] had now made his mark on SPL as the first West player to win pro, but this was not the end of Season 6, as [[player:blue|bLue]], [[player:graves|Graves]], [[player:barbeque|Barbeque]] and [[player:cole|Cole]] would go on to capture the Pacific Cup Championship, making [[player:bLue|bLue]] the first player to win both in the same season.

When asked what his favorite moment of his illustrious career was, [[player:bLue|bLue]] pointed all the way back to this very first Pro Championship, when they “beat out possibly the best team ever created in the [[team:pittsburgh_vipers|Pittsburgh Vipers]] (enry, cff, milk) in semi finals” before going on to win the finals with 100+ ping.
[[player:bLue|bLue]] wasn’t just winning, he was writing history.
paradise lost

For the next few seasons, [[player:blue|bLue]]’s career would begin to fall into a lull as he remained out of the spotlight, winning a bench Pro Championship in Season 7, getting his first 100 point pro season in Season 8 and then finally getting a bench West chip in Season 9. It seemed like bLue’s time as a top playoff performer was fading, as players like Milk and Jagr continued to blossom.

Season 11 looked to be no different as [[player:bLue|bLue]] only played in 4 regular season Pro games across 2 rosters, ending on the [[team:knoxville_kittens|Knoxville Kittens]]’ roster as they entered the playoffs. Once the playoffs began, something changed.
the return of the king

After multiple seasons spent outside the spotlight, [[player:bLue|bLue]] looked determined to remind the league who he was. Hungry for more success, he led not only the [[team:knoxville_kittens|Knoxville Kittens]] to a Pro Championship, but also the [[team:boston_blades|Boston Blades]] to a Cluster Cup and the [[team:provo_polar_pears|Provo Polar Bears]] to a Pacific Cup Championship.

Back at the top, [[player:blue|bLue]]’s thirst for more was not quenched as he would be one of the founding fathers of the soon-to-be [[team:long_island_lightning|Long Island Lightning]] dynasty. There, [[player:bLue|bLue]] would set his career high in Pro points with a 124 point season in 14 regular season games. To no one’s surprise, [[player:bLue|bLue]] would once again win both Pro and West, the fourth time both had been won in the same season, the third time [[player:bLue|bLue]] did it.
Dawn of SPL Central

Come Spring of 2024, excitement spread around the league, SPL was going to introduce a Central league that would bring the best of both regions together, an idea proposed by [[player:bLue|bLue]] many years earlier. Coming off a career high, all eyes were on [[player:bLue|bLue]] as now he’d have a chance to play against East players at a more equalized ping level. A man whose career has been full of firsts had the opportunity to once again make SPL history and win the Central division. Alongside [[player:zesh|Zesh]], [[player:kajun|Kajun]], [[player:voltzy|Voltzy]] and [[player:leon|Leon]], [[player:bLue|bLue]] and the [[team:shediac_lobsters|Shediac Lobsters]] would find themselves as the first seed going into the Central playoffs, where they would lose only one game on their way to be crowned SPL’s first Gazz Cup champions. This same season, one of my favorite moments that is often never talked about is [[player:bLue|bLue]] and the [[team:provo_polar_bear|Provo Polar Bear]]’s reverse sweep in the finals over the [[team:minneapolis_milk_duds|Milk Duds]] as [[player:bLue|bLue]] earned his 7th Pacific Cup Championship.
Fast forward 3 seasons, 2 Central Championships and a West Championship later we arrive at the very moment highlighted in the beginning of this story,
The Season 17 Finals

[[player:bLue|bLue]] having not played in the Pro Division at all in Season 16 and now teaming with one of the newest Pro players, [[player:chaos|Chaos]], had done it once again, bringing [[team:new_england_stars|New England]] and SPL to their feet as he won his fifth Pro Championship. [[player:bLue|bLue]] had dethroned the [[team:long_island_lightning|Long Island Lightning]] that he helped create, a team that had won four of the last five SPL Championships and were led by [[player:milk|Milk]]’s historic 106 goals and 170 points. This Championship seemed impossible going into the match-up. [[team:new_england_stars|New England]] seemed heavily outmatched. The [[team:long_island_lightning|Lightning]] had multiple seasons of experience together and had won together many times, [[player:milk|Milk]] had yet to be beaten in a Finals, but the [[team:new_england_stars|Stars]] roster was in their first season together and [[player:chaos|Chaos]] was not the established superstar he is today.
That night, [[player:bLue|bLue]] became the hero for most of SPL.

Nothing else mattered, the [[team:long_island_lightning|Lightning]] were beatable and new players were entering the scene. Like the legendary veteran leader he is, [[player:bLue|bLue]] shaped and molded [[player:chaos|Chaos]] into the very dagger he used to bring down SPL’s giants with just 52 seconds left. [[player:blue|Blue]]’s historic season did not end there though. He would follow up his Pro Championship with a Gaz Cup and a Pacific Cup, marking the first time a player had won all three within a single season, a feat that today is only held by two people.
[[player:Milk]] and [[player:bLue|bLue]] have gone head to head for as long as SPL can remember.
[[player:blue|bLue’s]] defense, ability to read plays before they happen and being able to get hard shots off from tough angles,

have always stood out to [[player:Milk]] during their long rivalry. When asked if there was ever a point where [[player:Milk]] realized that [[player:bLue|bLue]] would be one of the all-time greats he said,
I always thought he was one of the all-time greats since my first 2 seasons in Pro.

When you win that big, it is extremely hard to ever replicate it, something [[player:bLue|bLue]] knows best. Since Season 17, [[player:blue|bLue’s]] legacy has become intertwined with being a runner-up, as he has failed to add another Pro Championship to his collection in the 4 seasons since then. That is not to say there are not any bright spots in his career since then. [[player:blue|bLue’s]] goal in overtime game 3 of the Pro Finals as the [[team:new_england_stars|Stars]] began what was almost a reverse sweep might still be the most electric moment in recent history, if you know what goal I’m talking about, you understand. On top of that, [[player:bLue|bLue]] has won every West season and 2 of the 4 Central seasons since Season 17.
DarthTaksil best summed up [[player:blue|bLue’s]] career in a single sentence,
If you had only watched one final. There is a 38% chance [[player:bLue|bLue]] was winning it.
Retirement?

While Season 22 is now beginning to be underway, the status of [[player:blue|bLue’s]] career is up in the air. It is hard to ignore what a dominant career [[player:bLue|bLue]] has put together, as he has little left to prove. Twenty one seasons and 34 unique teams later, across all regions and divisions, regular season and playoffs combined, [[player:bLue|bLue]] holds the all-time records for games played, goals, assists and points of any player in SPL history. He stands 1,154 points ahead of [[player:jagr|Jagr]] for the SPL all-time lead in points, a record that is going to take quite a while to beat. Whether you count his “bench” chips or not, [[player:bLue|bLue]] is still the only player to have hit 20+ championships, with 21 officially and 24 unofficially, another record that is going to take a long time to surpass. Yet the story may not be over. While the future of his career remains uncertain, his place in SPL history does not.

The goal with 52 seconds left will always be remembered. But what made that moment special wasn't the shot itself, it was everything that came before it. [[player:bLue|bLue]] shaped SPL history over and over again, showing players who aren’t based directly on the East Coast that they too could compete at the highest levels.

There have been plenty of great players in SPL history, but very few have changed the direction of the league itself. [[player:bLue|bLue]] did just that. He proved West players could dominate at the highest level, helped pave the way for Central, and won everywhere he went.
Long before he scored the biggest goal of Season 17, he had already changed what players believed was possible.
